title: Consistency Models
summary: Strong consistency, linearizability, sequential consistency, causal consistency, eventual consistency — with concrete examples.

sections.content:
## The Consistency Spectrum

Consistency models define what **guarantees** a distributed system provides about the ordering and visibility of reads and writes. From strongest to weakest:

1. **Linearizability** (Strict consistency)
2. **Sequential consistency**
3. **Causal consistency**
4. **Eventual consistency**

Stronger models are easier to reason about but have higher latency and lower availability.

Consistency"]

Consistency

    style Strong fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style Eventual fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Linearizability

The strongest model. Operations appear **instantaneous** and respect real-time ordering — as if there's a single global timeline.

If write W completes before read R starts, R must see W's value.

```
Time ─────────────────────────────────────────▶

Thread 1:  [write x=1 ─────────]
                                    [read x=1 ✓]
Thread 2:                       [read x=1 ✓]

// Any read that starts AFTER the write completes must return 1
// This is the bank account guarantee
```

**Used by**: ZooKeeper, etcd, Spanner, CockroachDB
**Cost**: Requires coordination (Paxos, Raft) → higher latency

## Causal Consistency

Operations that are **causally related** appear in causal order. Concurrent operations (no causal relationship) can appear in any order.

```
// Causally related: B is a reply to A
Post:  Alice posts message A
Reply: Bob replies to A with B (B causally depends on A)

// Any reader who sees B must also see A
// Concurrent posts (no causal link) can appear in any order
```

**Used by**: MongoDB (causal sessions), CockroachDB causal reads, collaborative editing (Git)

**Practical example**: "Like" on a comment — you should see the comment before the like (causal). But two independent comments can appear in any order.

## Eventual Consistency

The weakest model. If no new updates are made to an item, eventually all reads will return the same (latest) value. No timing guarantee.

```
Time 0: Write x=1 to Node A
Time 0: Read x from Node B → returns 0 (stale) ← allowed!
Time 0.5: Replication propagates
Time 1: Read x from Node B → returns 1 (consistent)
```

**Used by**: Cassandra (default), DynamoDB (default), DNS, CDN caches

### Eventual Consistency Variants:
- **Read-your-writes**: You always see your own writes
- **Monotonic reads**: You never see older data after seeing newer data
- **Session consistency**: Within a session, read-your-writes + monotonic reads

sections.title: Eventual Consistency — Replication Convergence
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant N1 as Node 1
    participant N2 as Node 2
    C->>N1: write x=1
    N1-->>C: ACK
    C->>N2: read x
    N2-->>C: x=0 (stale! not yet replicated)
    Note over N1,N2: Async replication...
    N1->>N2: replicate x=1
    C->>N2: read x
    N2-->>C: x=1 (eventually consistent)

rows.Guarantee: All ops appear instantaneous, real-time ordered
All ops in some sequential order per thread
Causally related ops ordered, concurrent unordered
All replicas converge given no new writes

sections.question: Which consistency model guarantees that if you see a reply to a post, you will also see the original post?
sections.explanation: Causal consistency guarantees that causally related operations appear in causal order. A reply causally depends on the original post — you must have seen the post to reply to it. Any reader who sees the reply is guaranteed to also see the original post. Eventual consistency makes no such guarantee — you might see the reply before the post.
